item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't quite understand it but I like it. I'll go with the "fae circle" theory because it sounds logical, at least to me. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author used latin subclasses when categorizing his articles. "Nuntius" means "messenger".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Argh, it's frustrating! This SCP IS very interesting and I was glad to see it goes at last slightly beyond the usual "object makes people obsessed with it" stuff (in fact, I find it intriguing that humans are NOT affected by this, especially since they are the only ones affected by another property of the SCP) but it lacks a good payoff.

It feels like it has a lot of potential and should build up to something really striking but it doesn't really happen. Don't get me wrong, it's good to keep the audience guessing but it feels like it should give us slightly more material so we can really speculate as to what's going on here. It doesn't have to be an explicit explanation (in fact, I'd rather have it not be explicit), but at least some sort of implication.

As it stands, we can't do much more than really observe the facts but we can't really put them together in a coherent whole. I mean, I guess this thing monitors and/or predicts nuclear warfare activities, but to what end? And how is it related to the effect on animals (and why only vertebrates)?

I'll give it a +1 for originality and because it is well written but damn, I really feel it could become a classic if it said just a tiiiiiny little bit more. Basically, it's frustrating because it's good when it could be great.

I'm also confused as to what "Euclid-nuntii" is supposed to mean as it's never explained in the article itself and so it feels like it's only here for the sake of being different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very Leak
Much answer
So upvote
Wow

item.title: NEW PHOTO ASSHOLES
item.content:encoded:
I just couldn't bear the thought of this one without a photo, so I got a new one! And it's great! Original is here.

© Copyright Thomas Wood and licensed for reuse under this Creative Commons Licence.

item.title: Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Photo Credit: © Chris Drury – http://www.chrisdrury.co.uk

See new photo credit below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the footnote that casually acknowledges the regular use of nuclear fusion for the generation of electrical power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feex!

And to answer your other question, this is actually my only article that was based largely off of a picture. I combined it with an idea I had of an area that animals would just quietly sit down and look at like they were waiting (the hook being "what are they waiting for" or something like that), and I just fleshed it out until I hit the optimal conservation of WTF.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You said DD/MM/YYYY but your next date is in the opposite format, so I edited it; feel free to revert.

Also, I like it. It's one of those "strange" SCPs where I wonder exactly what was going through the author's head when they made it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry I'm posting on an old thread just to nitpick, but is 07/30/2071 correctly formatted given that the line above that says the dates are in DD/MM/YYYY format? Shouldn't it be 30/07/2071?

I do really like the idea of some mysterious thing that really does not like nuclear explosions — upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I got the reference as soon as I read this. Unfortunately, I only read this just now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ssshhhh no tears

only dreams now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great and puny minds sometimes think alike.

If I elaborate no further on that, each of us can imagine that we came out on top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny. A little while ago I was thinking about making a counter-movement art SCP that would feature that exact response, which is why I first came to such suspicions, but I didn't think about vocalizing those suspicions until now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspiciously specifically deny making a very subtle AWCY? reference that nobody ever got on their own which bugged me since I thought it was kinda clever myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"No, and we never will be."

I'm starting to think that this is a response to a certain question that gets asked around here…

Sorry for bringing that up, but maybe it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly this is an exception to the rule, and only the Overseers know why that exception exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Either the year 2071 needs to be changed, or the date should be "no more than two centuries in the future".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this better before I got to the bit about the nukes; before that, it was intriguingly puzzling, and after that, I felt like I understood what it was about.

Edit: actually, the bit about solar flares doesn't quite make sense. (I mean that in a good way.) There's clearly a connection between Earth fission/fusion and solar fission/fusion, but nuclear power plants *don't* trigger it… are solar flares some sort of weapon?

Needs more emphasis of the fact that we really don't understand this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I knew that picture would get used, I shall now take credit for the majority of this article (mentally).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I FEEX

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only affects non-humans. Interesting that this ties in with fae myth, though; I've never been familiar with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except it doesn't affect humans. Which is, as I see it, both a good and a bad thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description of how people looking at the circle end up standing still and not aging reminded me of the fairy rings from old stories, where you'd wander in and take a nap and wake up to find that hundreds of years had passed.

Then the description of the nuclear effects came and you blew my mind. I've decided in my headcanon that the fair folk are not happy with what mankind is doing with the atom.

Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems interesting.
It has little background and explaining as to why it affects non-human vertebrates are affected and why only them. (Even theories might have been nice.)
However it still seems like a new refreshing SCP almost like it was naturally made on the planet. Also does the effect of it going deeper into the earth not affect it's containment? like making it deeper and ect?
Maybe a few edits would be nice but I like the SCP in general overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's part of what I like about this, that we feel like we're waiting for the big effects to kick in. It's nuclear related, but doesn't shoot radiation…it's nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing's made of lots of blocks of slate; there's no good way of getting it out of all the crevasses short of water or a vacuum. It's not that they can't have anyone exposed to it ever, it's just that they've decided it's safest all around to keep anything coming out of this thing at arm's distance, just in case it does some nasty shit that they don't know about yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: TOO LATE DOWNVOTED

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wikidot's derping hard, won't let anyone edit it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strange, weird and wonderful—but no mainlist name?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, the current procedure used to collect the ash seems over the top. I'd think that having it be swept every day by level-0 personnel or D-Class personnel would be sufficient. From the description, just wearing a dust filter mask and goggles would be enough protection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weirdly compelling. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done Scantron! I think I shall be adding this to my bookmark of favorites, I really liked it! +111

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a boyfriend.

I'm not suggesting a four way though, however…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, first the tale about my Proposal, and now this. I do have a girlfriend, you know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this.

I really love this.

I'm going to go try and make an audio file of the sounds SCP-1246 makes now. It'll take me a couple of weeks but it'll be done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it have some connection to meteor strikes?

Lol i unno

It might be better to state this as the number of cm^3 or m^3 generated per day/week/month/year.

I'd considered that, but hadn't gotten around to it. Changing now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
with trace amounts of iridium accumulates

Does it have some connection to meteor strikes?

Through an unknown process, a mixture consisting primarily of volcanic ash with trace amounts of iridium accumulates on SCP-1246 at an approximate rate of 30cm/yr.

So, enough ash is generated each year to cover the entire circle to a depth of 30cm. It might be better to state this as the number of cm^3 or m^3 generated per day/week/month/year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what we in the biz call a typo. Good catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1246 is contained in a cylindrical tank of water thirteen meters in diameter and one meter in diameter.

What? Aside from that, I've got no strong feelings one way or the other. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool to see one of my pictures being used. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, almost forgot to do this. Thanks to people in several chats for looking this over and helping me get rid of the stupid, particularly Reject who did an especially thorough review of it in a PM.

EDIT: And Roget for the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work, Scantron. Still wonder what's up with the sun and the nuclear explosions, but I like the implications.

item.title: O.O
item.content:encoded: I like this.
